coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorometric detection of para-nitrophenol from its isomers by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fengxiang
Fu, Xinyang
Chai, Xinyue
Han, Qiming
Wang, Hua
Hao, Qingli
coredata.dc:description:
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with blue fluorescence and excellent water-solubility and an average diameter of approximately 21 nm have been prepared via hydrothermal treatment of ammonium fluoroborate and trisodium citrate, with the average size in diameter about 21 nm. It was found that para-nitrophenol (4-NP) could dramatically quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs via static quenching because the good linearity of the Stern-Volmer equation (
k q
≫ 2.0 × 1010 L/(mol·s)) and the change in lifetime before and after the addition of 4-NP, which are different from that of its isomers and other analogs or metal ions. A fast and direct fluorimetric method with N-GQDs probe has thereby been developed for detecting 4-NP at concentrations from 0 to 20 μM, with a limit of detection of 0.29 μM. Furthermore, this proposed fluorimetric method was successfully applied to analyze 4-NP in real water samples, showing the feasible detection of trace 4-NP in complicated environmental water samples. The developed N-GQDs-based fluorimetric method is expected to be applied to the highly selective determination of 4-NP from its isomers, other analogs and general metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen-doping
Fluorimetric analysis
Para-nitrophenol detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of 4-NP in real water samples

R. China, the program Foundation for Science and Technology of Changzhou, China (CZ20190001), the program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, and the ‘333 program’ BRA2019262 of Jiangsu Province, China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for TEM, AFM, XRD and FTIR data collection.

R. China, the program Foundation for Science and Technology of Changzhou, China (CZ20190001), the program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, and the ‘333 program’ BRA2019262 of Jiangsu Province, China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for TEM, AFM, XRD and FTIR data collection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for N-GQDs and possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of sensing 4-NP.

TEM image (a), XRD pattern (b), AFM image (c), cross-section height profile (d) of N-GQDs, FT-IR spectra of ammonium fluoride, sodium citrate and N-GQDs (e), the wide scan XPS spectrum (f), the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra (g) and the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra (h) of N-GQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption, emission (fixed at 330 nm) and excitation (fixed at 420 nm) spectra of the N-GQDs; (b) emission spectra of N-GQDs under different excitation wavelength.

(a) Emission spectra of N-GQDs with different amounts of 4-NP (from top to bottom, 4-NP concentrations of 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 150, and 300 μM. (b) Reciprocal of the relative intensity (F 0 / F) of N-GQDs versus the concentration of 4-NP (inset: F 0 / F of N-GQDs versus the concentrations of 4-NP from 0 to 20 μM). (c) The relative fluorescence intensity (F / F 0) of N-GQDs in the presence and absence of various possible interferents (including their analogs and representative metal ions) (black column: only the presence of 4-NP or interferents, red column: both the presence of 4-NP and interferents).

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorometric detection of para-nitrophenol from its isomers by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, Nanjing 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, Nanjing 210094, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondings author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China (F. Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with blue fluorescence and excellent water-solubility and an average diameter of approximately 21 nm have been prepared via hydrothermal treatment of ammonium fluoroborate and trisodium citrate, with the average size in diameter about 21 nm. It was found that para-nitrophenol (4-NP) could dramatically quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs via static quenching because the good linearity of the Stern-Volmer equation (
k q
≫ 2.0 × 1010 L/(mol·s)) and the change in lifetime before and after the addition of 4-NP, which are different from that of its isomers and other analogs or metal ions. A fast and direct fluorimetric method with N-GQDs probe has thereby been developed for detecting 4-NP at concentrations from 0 to 20 μM, with a limit of detection of 0.29 μM. Furthermore, this proposed fluorimetric method was successfully applied to analyze 4-NP in real water samples, showing the feasible detection of trace 4-NP in complicated environmental water samples. The developed N-GQDs-based fluorimetric method is expected to be applied to the highly selective determination of 4-NP from its isomers, other analogs and general metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of fluorescence carbon nanoparticles in 2004 [1], fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, carbon dots or carbon quantum dots (CDs) [2], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [3]) have attracted increasing attention as novel fluorescent materials. In particular, due to the special property of carbon materials, the pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects [4], GQDs can have ultralow toxicity, good solubility and stability, stable fluorescence, and high electrical and thermal conductivity, these qualities has ignited tremendous research interest [5].

Until now, the preparation methods of GQDs have mainly included two main routes: top-down and bottom-up routes. The top-down route mainly involves the oxidative [6] and reductive cutting of bulk graphitized carbon [7]. The bottom-up route involves organic synthesis [8], carbonization of organic precursors [9] and fusion of organic molecules [10,11]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal method is very popular because of its simple manipulation and mild reaction condition requirements. Moreover, the bottom-up methods can offer exciting opportunities to control GQDs with well-defined sizes, shapes, and special excellent properties [12]. In addition, doping heteroatoms into carbon materials can effectively tune their intrinsic properties [13]. Hence, heteroatom-doped GQDs have attracted great attention [14–16], particularly in the development of fluorescent probes. In addition, the different doping forms of heteroatoms brought by different preparation methods will bring special properties, which is conducive to expanding the application field of doped GQDs. Thus, it is necessary to prepare new doped GQDs and search for their fluorescent properties by controlling the doped-GQDs composition.

Para-nitrophenol (4-NP), a common and significant aromatic nitro-compound, is widely utilized as a raw material or additive in the explosives, pesticides, dyes, medicines, and plastics industries including the hydrolysis product of some organophosphorus insecticides [17]. 4-NP has two isomers, 3-nitrophenol and 2-nitrophenol, which have different chemical properties due to the different nitrogen positions. In addition, para-nitrophenol, a refractory organic environmental pollutant, can significantly damage the ecological environment and impact biological health because of its toxicity [18]. In particular, ingestion of 4-NP may cause obvious discomfort, including headache, drowsiness, nausea, and cyanosis [19,20]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) permits a maximum limit of 60 mg/L (0.43 μM) for drinking water [21]. Therefore, it is of great importance to accurately detect 4-NP in water samples. In particular, the specific identification of 4-NP from its isomers is a formidable problem. To date, there are some methods for detecting 4-NP including spectrophotometry, liquid chromatography, electrochemistry, photoelectrochemistry, and fluorescence [22–26]. Unfortunately, most of them may have some disadvantages, such as complicated and time-consuming operation procedures, relatively high-cost equipment. Thus, it is still necessary to develop an environmentally friendly, effective and reliable sensor for the low-cost, simple and rapid detection of 4-NP in water samples.

In the present work, water-stable N-GQDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of ammonium fluoroborate and trisodium citrate. In contrast to its isomers, other analogs and metal ions, 4-NP can noticeably quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs. They are thereby employed as fluorescent probes for selectively detecting 4-NP from its isomers. The quenching mechanism of the developed detection system was studied mainly by static quenching (as shown in Fig. 1 ). Subsequently, the developed fluorimetric method was successfully used for 4-NP assay in real water samples, demonstrating that N-GQDs have great potential for wide practical application in the monitoring of environmental pollutants.

Ammonium fluoroborate (H4BF4N, 99.5%), trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7, 98%), quinine sulphate ((C20H24N2O2)2·H2SO4··2H2O, 98.6%), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, 98%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 ~ 38%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP, 99%), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP, 98%), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP, 98%), phenol (99%), hydroquinone (99%) and nitrobenzene (NB, 99%), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4, 99%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4, 99%) and metal salts were purchased from commercial source of analytical reagent grade. All the chemicals used in this paper were used directly without further purification. Deionized water (18 MΩ·cm) was used throughout all experiments.

X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250, America), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, ThermoFisher Nicolet IS-10, America), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan), atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon, Germany), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) were utilized for the characterization of the N-GQDs. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were respectively recorded using UV-1201 spectrophotometer (China) and FL3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon, France). Fluorescence decay curves were carried out with the time-correlated single-photon counting techniques (TCSPC, Jobin Yvon, France) at room temperature.

N-GQDs were prepared by following a facile and bottom-up hydrothermal route below. Briefly, 2 g H4BF4N and 1 g C6H5Na3O7 were added into 25 mL deionized water to be dissolved ultrasonically. Then, the solution was heated at 200 °C for 5 h in a polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL). The mixture was then centrifuged (12 000 rpm, rotor: H0810, 30 min) to achieve the suspension after cooling to room temperature. And the suspension was further dialyzed (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 24 h. The blue fluorescent N-GQDs solution was obtained, and the solid product of N-GQDs was dried through a freeze-drying process. The investigations on the main synthesis conditions of N-GQDs was shown Fig. S1.

The N-GQDs-based fluorimetric detection process of 4-NP was carried out at room temperature. Standard solution of 4-NP (0.01 M) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of 4-NP in water and the volume was fixed to 10.0 mL in a volumetric flask. Other solutions of different concentrations were prepared by dilution. An aliquot of 2.0 mL fixed concentration of N-GQDs (1.0 mg/mL) was as the basic testing solution, then the fluorescent spectrum of the solution was recorded from 350 to 640 nm with excitation wavelength fixed at 330 nm. And the fluorescent spectra of the mixed solution with different concentration of 4-NP was again recorded after the mixed solutions were equilibrated for 30 s. The detection process of other compounds or ions were performed with similar procedures.

The developed N-GQDs-based fluorimetric method was utilized to analyze 4-NP in real water samples (tap water and river water). After tap water samples containing different amounts of 4-NP were added into the analysis system, the fluorescence spectra were respectively measured. The river water samples were treated by the following two steps for the removal of suspended particles, which were collected from the Liao River located in the south of Qufu City. It was first filtered with 0.45 μm microfiltration membrane. Under the same conditions, the experiments were repeated for 5 times to calculate the relative standard deviation (RSD).

The TEM image, XRD pattern, AFM image, cross-section height profile of the N-GQDs, FT-IR spectra of ammonium fluoride, sodium citrate and N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 2 a, b, c and d. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the average size of the N-GQDs is approximately 21 nm (<100 nm [3]), which might be mainly distributed in the range of 17–25 nm. The data suggest that the N-GQDs are uniformly dispersed, showing good water solubility without ultrasonic dispersion. As shown in Fig. 2 b, the XRD diffraction pattern of the N-GQDs shows an obvious abroad diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 23.5°, which corresponds to the (002) reflection of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder, the presence of this peak reveals that the prepared N-GQDs have a disordered graphene structure [27]. Additionally, the AFM image (Fig. 2 c and d) shows a typical topographic height of 3–4 nm, indicating that the N-GQDs are present in the form of flakes rather than particles [28,29].

The FT-IR spectra of ammonium fluoride, sodium citrate and N-GQDs are given in Fig. 2 e. Compared with the FT-IR spectra of ammonium fluoride and sodium citrate, the characteristic peaks of sodium citrate and ammonium fluoroborate almost disappeared in the N-GQDs samples, indicating the formation of new products. As shown in Fig. 2 e, a strong vibration peak at approximately 1563 cm−1 is attributed to C = C stretching [30,31], suggesting that the N-GQDs present the structure of graphene. The peak at approximately 3400 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds or N–H bonds, whereas the peak at approximately 1699 cm−1 indicates the existence of COO− [32]. The absorption bands at approximately 1402 cm−1 and 1176 cm−1 can be assigned to C-O-C stretching vibrations [27,33]. The band at approximately 1360 cm−1 may be attributed to C-N stretching vibrations [27]. The results indicate that the N-GQDs are successfully doped by nitrogen atoms, and that their main structure is graphene sheet, which is rich in functional groups including hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups [34].

The functional substructures and sub-compositions of the N-GQDs were further studied by XPS. Fig. 2 f, g, and h show the wide scan XPS spectrum, the high-resolution C 1 s and the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of N-GQDs, respectively. The wide scan XPS spectrum of the N-GQDs exhibits three peaks at 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV, 532.0 eV, which should belong to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively, while a peak at 1072.0 eV is attributed to Na 1 s [35,36], as shown in Fig. 2 f. The results indicate that carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sodium should be present at the surface of N-GQDs with an atomic ratio of 68.47: 28.57: 4.07: 2.89 (as shown in Table S1). Additionally, the presence of sodium may confirm the presence of carboxylate groups in the N-GQDs. In addition, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. 2 g) indicates the presence of C = C (284.8 eV), C-OH (286.0 eV), C = O or C-N (288.6 eV) [35,37]. Of note, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 h) indicates the presence of C-N-C (400.1 eV) and N-(C)3 (401.0 eV) bonds [35,37]. Therefore, the XPS results further confirm that nitrogen was successfully doped on the N-GQDs, which is in good agreement with the information from the FT-IR mentioned above (Fig. 2 e).

From the FT-IR and XPS analyses, we speculate that the N-GQDs may have an abundance of functional groups on the surface of the N-GQDs, such as oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. In particular, the presence of these groups is very important for their good stability and dispersion, which can play a key role in the application of fluorescent probes.

Fig. S2 shows the photographs of water and N-GQDs solutions taken under visible and UV light (365 nm). In this figure, the blue fluorescence can be observed by the naked eye. Furthermore, the optical properties of the N-GQDs, mainly including the three-dimensional projection (Fig. S3), the steady-state fluorescence and UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3 ), were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. S3, the N-GQDs present strong fluorescence in the excitation range of 310 nm to 350 nm, which belongs to the blue light region. In addition, the fluorescence becomes weaker with increasing excitation wavelength.

Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the N-GQDs. An absorption band at 330 nm might be attributed to the n-π* transitions of functional groups in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs [38,39], which indicates that the N-GQDs are easily excited. Moreover, the maximum emission peak is at 420 nm when excited at the strongest excitation peak (330 nm). The N-GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior as shown in Fig. 3 b. Importantly, the fluorescent behavior of the N-GQDs is similar to that of some other fluorescent carbon materials [40], where the fluorescence behavior might be mainly caused by quantum confinement effects.

It is widely recognized that the good photostability of fluorescent materials is significant for their development and application. A spectral comparison of the initial fluorescence of the N-GQDs and the fluorescence after half a year is shown in Fig. S4 a. The fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged over time at room temperature without light protection, indicating stable fluorescence. Additionally, the N-GQDs may not need to be refrigerated and protected from light unlike semiconductor quantum dots. The tolerance for photobleaching of the N-GQDs was investigated under continuous Xenon lamp irradiation at 330 nm. The fluorescence intensity hardly changed after continuous excitation as shown in Fig. S4 b, indicating that the N-GQDs showed excellent photostability. These results indicate that N-GQDs have good fluorescence stability and light quenching resistance, which makes it possible for N-GQDs to be used as fluorescent probes.

To the best of our knowledge, the fluorescence properties of different molecules and nanoparticles may be affected by ionic strength and pH [41]. To investigate the salt tolerance of the developed N-GQDs, the fluorescent properties of N-GQDs were studied at different concentrations of sodium chloride (Fig. S4 c). No obvious change was observed in the fluorescence emission intensity, suggesting that the effect of the ionic strength on the fluorescent properties of N-GQDs is negligible. Fig. S4 d shows the fluorescence intensity ratio of the N-GQDs at different pH, showing a varying relationship as the pH increases from 3 to 11. No obvious fluorescence intensity changes were found, and only a certain degree of quenching was observed in the range of low pH values. After the possible influence of pH change was considered, PBS buffer solution was selected as the dispersion solution for the N-GQDs.

In conclusion, the N-GQDs can exhibit good optical stability and excellent tolerance to high ionic strength, which may make it possible to provide practical applications in some specialized fields, including analysis, cell imaging and fluorescent labeling.

The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs solutions with different concentrations of 4-NP are recorded in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence of the N-GQDs can be efficiently quenched by 4-NP, and the value of the relative fluorescence intensity (
F /
F 0
, where
F 0
and F are the FL intensities at 330 nm in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively) was reduced to approximately 30% of its initial value as the amount of 4-NP accumulated to 20 μM. The relative fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs became very weak when the amount of 4-NP accumulated to 300 μM. Additonally, the relationship between the reciprocal of relative fluorescence intensity and the concentration of 4-NP is demonstrated in Fig. 4 b. From 0 to 20 μM, the reciprocal of
F /
F 0
versus the concentration of 4-NP shows good linearity as shown in Fig. 4 b. The limit of detection (LOD) of 4-NP was calculated to be 0.29 μM (
3
S b
/ k
) in this analysis system [31].

To evaluate the performance of the N-GQDs-based probe, the proposed method of 4-NP detection was compared with those in published works, and the related data are listed in Table S2. The data show that this method has an acceptable LOD, that is lower than the standard for drinking water permitted by EPA [21]. Therefore, the N-GQDs can potentially be used as a probe for the detection of 4-NP.

High selectivity is another important index of the probe. Herein, the fluorescent quenching of the N-GQDs was studied in the presence of various possible interferents including their analogs and representative metal ions (3-NP, 2-NP, 4-CP, NB, phenol, hydroquinone, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+) at the same concentration. Fig. 4 c shows the values of relative fluorescence intensity (
F /
F 0
), where
F 0
and F at 330 nm refer to the absence and presence of interferents, respectively (black column: only the presence of 4-NP or interferents, red column: both the presence of 4-NP and interferents). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 c, 4-NP can effectively quench the fluorescence of the N-GQDs, the value of
F /
F 0
decreases by approximately 35% with 25 μM 4-NP, and other nitrophenol isomers barely quench the fluorescence of the N-GQDs. Therefore, the developed N-GQD probe can distinguish 4-NP from its isomers. In addition, other kinds of interferents (analogs and general metal ions) cannot quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs. We also studied the responses of the N-GQDs probes to 4-NP in the presence of other interferents (Fig. 4 c red column). The results show that these interferents did not interfere with the detection of 4-NP, indicating that this method shows high selectivity in the detection of 4-NP.

The type of fluorescent quenching of N-GQDs is thought to be mainly dynamic (collisional deactivation) or static quenching (complexation), which can be described by the Stern-Volmer Eqs. (1) and (2) [42].
(1)
F 0
/ F =
τ 0
/ τ = 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where
F 0
and F are the fluorescence intensities at 330 nm in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively, and
τ 0
and τ are the lifetimes of the fluorescent materials in the absence and presence of the quenchers, respectively. [Q] and
k q
are the quencher concentration and the constant of dynamic (collisional) quenching, respectively.
K
SV
is the constant of static quenching in Eq. (2). In particular, the lifetime is unchanged for static quenching in the absence and presence of quenchers, where the value of
τ 0
/ τ
is equal to 1. Therefore, the change in lifetime before and after the addition of the quenchers is an important indicator to discriminate between dynamic and static quenching [42].

To explore the possible quenching mechanism in the developed analysis system, the Stern-Volmer plot of our analysis system was fitted. Fig. S5 a shows the Stern-Volmer plot of the quenching experiments (
F 0
/ F
versus [4-NP]). As shown in Fig. S5 a, the fitted result has good linearity (R2 = 0.99945, 0–20 μM) and is in accordance with the Stern-Volmer equation (
F 0
/ F = 0.01761
[ Q ]
+ 0.99509
), so the quenching type in this analysis system may be static quenching or dynamic quenching. Where the value of
K
SV
is 17,610 L/mol, the corresponding value of
k q
is 1.761 × 1012 L/(mol·s), and
k q
≫ 2.0 × 1010 L/(mol·s). The influence of temperature on the Stern-Volmer plots was also investigated (Table S3),
K
SV
(25 °C) >
K
SV
(37 °C). The results indicated that the quenching in this scenario was not dynamic [43].

To further identify the type of quenching, we studied the fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs before and after 4-NP by the following experiments. Fig. S5 b shows the fluorescence decay curves of the N-GQDs before and after introducing 4-NP (Ludox was used as Prompt). As shown in Fig. S5 b, the fluorescence decay curves of the N-GQDs are almost unchanged in the absence and presence of 4-NP, and the lifetime decays curves of the N-GQDs remain 2-exponential. The fluorescence lifetime remains almost the same before and after 4-NP addition, where
τ 1
and
τ 2
are 1.64 ns and 7.28 ns, respectively. The results of the unchanged lifetime indicate that the quenching mechanism is mainly static quenching (complexation) rather than dynamic quenching (collisional deactivation) in this case [31].

From the above results including the fitting of the Stern-Volmer equation and the fluorescence lifetime study, therefore, it is concluded that the quenching process by 4-NP should be static quenching in the low concentration range of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of 4-NP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the N-GQDs probe for 4-NP, real tap water and river water samples containing different amounts of 4-NP were tested with the N-GQDs-based analysis system. As 4-NP was not detected in the real water samples, the recovery was studied with the relevant results listed in Table S4. The experimental results of the recoveries and RSDs indicate that the proposed N-GQDs probe can be potentially applicable to detect trace 4-NP in real water samples.

In summary, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with blue fluorescence and excellent water solubility have been prepared as fluorescent 4-NP probes by a simple one-pot synthesis strategy. It was found that 4-NP can noticeably quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs. A rapid and reliable fluorimetric method has been developed for the detection of 4-NP at linear concentrations from 0 to 20 μM. The developed N-GQDs probes exhibit excellent aqueous stability and high sensing selectivity for 4-NP with a low limit of detection of 0.29 μM. Moreover, the detection results for 4-NP from river water and tap water samples indicate that the N-GQDs-based fluorimetric method can be potentially applicable for the detection of 4-NP in environmental water samples including drinking water. In addition, a static quenching mechanism is proposed for the current analytical system based on the Stern-Volmer equation and the results of the fluorescence lifetime study. Importantly, the developed N-GQDs-based fluorimetric method may find wide applications for the environmental monitoring of 4-NP with high selectivity and sensitivity, which can enable the determination 4-NP from its isomers, other analogs and general metal ions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Higher Educational Science and Technology Program of Shandong Province (No. J18KB054), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Nos.22074079and21675099), Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018ZC0129), Shandong, P. R. China, the program Foundation for Science and Technology of Changzhou, China (CZ20190001), the program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, and the ‘333 program’ BRA2019262 of Jiangsu Province, China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for TEM, AFM, XRD and FTIR data collection.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106389.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
